analysis:
- Common backbone: nondominated prefilter → cheap box/weight proxy scoring → build a k‑proportional shortlist → CELF lazy greedy using cached singleton HVs → bounded 1‑for‑1 swap refinement → deterministic padding. Stronger heuristics tune shortlist sizing, scoring blend, caching, and swap budget more carefully.
- Scoring: best ones blend rectilinear box volume with multiple scalarization probes (dirichlet/weights + mild transforms). This gives a cheap, discriminative ordering that reduces expensive HV work.
- Shortlist sizing: adaptive sizes proportional to k and pareto front size work better than fixed caps; too small shortlists force padding (loss) while too large waste HV calls.
- Caching: singleton HV caching is essential; selective pairwise caching for small shortlists materially reduces recomputation. CELF timestamping further minimizes exact HV evaluations.
- HV budget & pruning: explicit hv-call budgets and cheap upper bounds (box‑volume or singleton upper bounds) control runtime vs quality tradeoffs. Strong heuristics use them to stop or fallback gracefully.
- Local search: bounded deterministic swaps reliably improve results but must be limited and use cached or incremental HVs when possible to avoid combinatorial HV calls.
- Efficiency pitfalls in weaker variants: repeated Python-level membership checks (list membership, .remove), repeated vstack/array constructions, recomputing identical proxies, naïve O(N^2) dominance checks without vectorized masks, and overzealous HV budgets cause excessive runtime.
- Data reuse opportunities: preallocating selected buffers, maintaining boolean masks/sets for O(1) membership, using numpy vector ops for dominance/scoring, and reusing precomputed diffs avoid Python looping overhead and repeated memory churn.
- Determinism: consistent seeding and deterministic tie‑breakers are well handled in stronger heuristics—important for reproducible selection and debugging.
- Failure modes: overly aggressive pruning or too tight budgets can miss good points; excessive HV caching without budgets can blow runtime.
experience: Favor a hybrid score (box volume × multi‑weight scalarization), build an adaptive shortlist (k‑ and pareto‑aware), cache singleton HVs plus small pairwise tables, use CELF lazy greedy with box upper‑bound pruning, cap HV calls, then run a small bounded deterministic swap (incremental HV updates). Vectorize dominance/scoring, preallocate buffers, and avoid expensive Python list operations for membership to get better quality/runtime.
current_reflection: Favor a hybrid score (box volume × multi‑weight scalarization), build an adaptive shortlist (k‑ and pareto‑aware), cache singleton HVs plus small pairwise tables, use CELF lazy greedy with box upper‑bound pruning, cap HV calls, then run a small bounded deterministic swap (incremental HV updates). Vectorize dominance/scoring, preallocate buffers, and avoid expensive Python list operations for membership to get better quality/runtime.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.

Design next heuristics by enforcing an O(k) shortlist, always cache singleton HVs, use CELF lazy evaluation with timestamps, apply cheap box-volume upper bounds to prune candidates, enable pairwise caching only for very small shortlists, cap HV calls and swap iterations, and prefer deterministic stable ordering. These focused changes yield most HV-quality gain per expensive HV call.

Prioritize reducing exact HV calls: build a high-precision O(k) shortlist with cheap proxies, cache singleton (and small pairwise) HVs, use CELF lazy updates and box‑bound pruning, then run a tiny deterministic swap pass. Add an explicit HV-call budget with graceful fallback approximations and vectorized nondomination/precomputation to get consistent, fast, and high-quality k‑subsets.
bad_reflections: Always prefilter to nondominated points, vectorize box/weight scores, and build an O(k) shortlist. Cache singleton HVs (and pairwise only when shortlist≤~40). Use CELF lazy greedy with timestamps and box‑based upper bounds, limit total HV calls, maintain selected points in a preallocated array to avoid vstack churn, and use sets/masks for membership. Finish with a tiny bounded deterministic swap pass. Consider lightweight scalar HVC approximations if HV calls must be avoided.

Favor deterministic shortlists, cheap vectorized proxies, cached singleton (and tiny pairwise) HVs, and CELF lazy greedy with tight upper‑bound pruning and an explicit HV‑call budget. Add a small, cached incremental swap pass. Prioritize data reuse (precompute diffs/scores), avoid Python-level remove/linear searches in hot loops, and keep shortlist sizes O(k) to maximize HV value per expensive call.

Favor small, Pareto-aware shortlists and CELF lazy-greedy with cached singleton HVs. Use vectorized box/scalar proxies to rank candidates, selectively cache pairwise HVs only when list ≤ ~40, enforce an HV-call budget, and prune using cheap upper bounds. Preallocate buffers and minimize vstack/copy inside hot loops; prefer deterministic seeds and tie-breakers. These choices give best hypervolume per HV call.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: hybrid score, k-aware shortlist, Pareto-aware, singleton & small pairwise cache, CELF lazy greedy, box-bound pruning, HV-call cap, deterministic swap, vectorize, preallocate
- Advice: build an O(k) adaptive shortlist using hybrid box‑volume × multi‑weight scoring (Pareto‑aware), cache singleton HVs (and pairwise only for tiny shortlists), run CELF lazy greedy with box upper bounds and a strict HV-call budget, then apply a small bounded deterministic swap with incremental HV updates; vectorize dominance/scoring and preallocate buffers.
- Avoid: Python-level membership/removal in hot loops, large pairwise caches (>~40), nested HV recomputations, unbounded HV calls, and nondeterministic tie-breaking.
- Explanation: maximizes hypervolume gain per expensive HV call, reuses incremental work, prunes cheaply, and produces fast, stable, high-quality k-subsets.
